spots.name: Sumida River Fireworks
spots.note: Tokyo's most iconic fireworks — ~20,000 shells over the Sumida River. One of Japan's oldest hanabi events. Book riverside restaurants months ahead.

spots.name: Nagaoka Fireworks
spots.nameJa: 長岡まつり大花火大会
spots.note: One of Japan's top 3 fireworks festivals — famous for the Phoenix (鳳凰) spread 2km wide. A memorial for WWII victims. Tickets sell out within minutes.

spots.name: Omagari All-Japan Fireworks
spots.note: Japan's most prestigious fireworks competition. Pyrotechnists from across Japan compete. Considered the pinnacle of Japanese hanabi artistry.

spots.name: Tsuchiura All-Japan Fireworks
spots.nameJa: 土浦全国花火競技大会
spots.note: Autumn fireworks competition — one of Japan's three major competitions alongside Nagaoka and Omagari. Held over Lake Kasumigaura.

spots.name: PL Fireworks (Tondabayashi)
spots.note: 100,000 fireworks in a single night — one of Japan's largest displays. Visible from 10km away. Organized by Perfect Liberty Kyodan.

spots.name: Miyajima Water Fireworks
spots.note: Fireworks launched from the sea in front of Itsukushima Shrine's floating torii gate. One of Japan's most photogenic hanabi backdrops.

spots.name: Lake Suwa Fireworks
spots.nameJa: 諏訪湖祭湖上花火大会
spots.note: 40,000 fireworks reflected over Lake Suwa on the night of Obon. One of the largest fireworks shows in the Chubu region.

spots.name: Jingu Gaien Fireworks
spots.note: Inner-city Tokyo fireworks near Meiji Jingu — no river needed. Features J-pop performances. Very popular with younger crowds.

spots.name: Naniwa Yodogawa Fireworks
spots.note: Osaka's premier summer fireworks over the Yodo River. Famous for the 'Niagara' waterfall fireworks effect.

spots.name: Atami Sea Fireworks
spots.typicalDate: Multiple times per year (Feb, Apr, Jul, Aug, Oct, Dec)
spots.note: Unique: held year-round in Atami Bay. The bowl-shaped bay amplifies sound and creates a spectacular visual. Close to Tokyo — easy day trip.

spots.name: Lake Toya Long-Run Fireworks
spots.nameJa: 洞爺湖ロングラン花火大会
spots.note: Japan's most unique fireworks — launched over Lake Toya every single night for 6 months. Small but intimate; can be seen from lakeside ryokan.

spots.name: Edogawa Fireworks
spots.note: Held the same night as Sumida River Fireworks — a great alternative with fewer crowds. 14,000 fireworks along the Edogawa river.

spots.name: Gion Matsuri
spots.note: One of Japan's three greatest festivals. Giant decorated floats (yamaboko) parade through Kyoto. The evening Yoiyama stalls are equally unmissable.

spots.name: Tenjin Matsuri
spots.note: One of Japan's three great festivals. A river procession of 100+ boats, torchlight, and fireworks on the Okawa River. Starts at Osaka Tenmangu Shrine.

spots.name: Aomori Nebuta Matsuri
spots.note: Towering illuminated floats depicting warriors and mythological figures parade through Aomori city at night. One of Tohoku's most spectacular summer festivals.

spots.name: Akita Kanto Matsuri
spots.note: Performers balance 12-meter bamboo poles holding 46 lanterns (50kg total!) on their foreheads, shoulders, and hips. Incredible display of skill.

spots.name: Awa Odori
spots.note: Japan's largest dance festival — 100,000 dancers and 1.3M spectators. The famous chant: 'Dancing fools and watching fools — both are fools, so why not dance!'

spots.name: Sendai Tanabata Festival
spots.note: Japan's largest Tanabata — thousands of elaborate bamboo streamers (kazari) hang over Sendai's shopping arcades. A month after the traditional Jul 7 date.

spots.name: Hakata Gion Yamakasa
spots.note: Teams race giant decorated floats (kazariyama) through Fukuoka's streets in the pre-dawn Oiyama race. Over 770 years of history.

spots.name: Sanja Matsuri
spots.note: Tokyo's wildest festival — 100 mikoshi (portable shrines) carried through Asakusa by 2 million spectators. The energy is electric.

spots.name: Kanda Matsuri
spots.note: One of Tokyo's three great festivals — held in odd-numbered years only (next: 2027). Elaborate mikoshi procession through Akihabara and Nihonbashi. Dates back to 1600s.

spots.name: Aoi Matsuri
spots.note: One of Kyoto's three great festivals. A slow procession of 500 Heian-era costumed participants from Kyoto Imperial Palace to Kamigamo Shrine.

spots.name: Hakata Dontaku Port Festival
spots.nameJa: 博多どんたく港まつり
spots.note: Japan's largest attendance festival. Citizens in costume parade through Fukuoka on everyday objects — spatulas, bamboo branches, flower carts. Very joyful.

spots.name: Takayama Spring Festival
spots.note: Elaborate floats with mechanical puppets (karakuri) parade through Takayama's historic old town. One of Japan's most beautiful festival settings.

spots.name: Takayama Autumn Festival
spots.note: Autumn version of the Takayama festival — same spectacular floats but set against autumn foliage. Often considered the more beautiful of the two.

spots.name: Jidai Matsuri
spots.note: One of Kyoto's three great festivals. 2,000 participants in historical costumes spanning 1,200 years of history march from Kyoto Imperial Palace to Heian Shrine.

spots.name: Kurama Fire Festival
spots.note: One of Kyoto's most dramatic festivals. Villagers carry torches through the mountain village of Kurama at night. Same date as Jidai Matsuri — do both.

spots.name: Nagasaki Kunchi
spots.note: Unique festival showing Nagasaki's Chinese and Dutch heritage — dragon dances, Dutch ship floats, and exotic performances at Suwa Shrine.

spots.name: Kishiwada Danjiri Festival
spots.note: Wild and thrilling — teams pull heavy wooden floats (danjiri) at full speed through narrow streets. People ride on top performing acrobatics. Extremely intense.

spots.name: Chichibu Night Festival
spots.note: Japan's most spectacular winter festival — elaborate floats parade through snowy Chichibu at night with fireworks overhead. Named a UNESCO intangible heritage.

spots.name: Omizutori (Shunie)
spots.note: 1,260-year-old Buddhist rite at Todai-ji's Nigatsu-do. Monks wave giant blazing torches from the veranda — sparks are said to ward off evil. Free to view.

spots.name: Tanabata (Star Festival)
spots.note: Nationwide festival celebrating two stars (Vega & Altair) meeting once a year. People write wishes on paper strips (tanzaku) and hang on bamboo. Celebrated everywhere.

spots.name: Obon (Bon Festival)
spots.note: National holiday when ancestors' spirits return. Local bon odori dances, lantern floating (toro nagashi) on rivers/lakes, and family gatherings across Japan.

spots.name: Sanno Matsuri
spots.note: One of Tokyo's three great festivals — held in even-numbered years only (next: 2026). The grand procession passes through Akasaka and the Imperial Palace area.

spots.name: Yamagata Hanagasa Festival
spots.note: 10,000 dancers parade through Yamagata waving flower-decorated straw hats (hanagasa) to the rhythmic Yamagata Hanagasa song. One of Tohoku's five major festivals.

spots.name: Sapporo Snow Festival
spots.note: Japan's most famous winter event — over 200 massive snow and ice sculptures in Odori Park, including replicas of world landmarks. 2+ million visitors.

spots.name: Yokote Kamakura Festival
spots.note: Children build igloo-like kamakura snow houses and invite passersby in for sweet sake and rice cakes. Magical winter scene of 100+ glowing kamakura at night.

spots.name: Asahikawa Winter Festival
spots.note: Hokkaido's second-largest winter festival with giant snow sculptures, ice slides, and the world's largest snow sculpture competition.

spots.name: Shirakawa-go Winter Illuminations
spots.note: UNESCO World Heritage village lit up in winter. Snow-covered thatched-roof (gassho-zukuri) farmhouses glow against the night sky. Shuttle bus required — reserve well ahead.

spots.name: Nozawaonsen Dosojin Fire Festival
spots.note: One of Japan's three great fire festivals. A dramatic battle between young men defending a sacred shrine made of wood and torchbearers trying to set it alight.

spots.name: Otaru Snow Light Path Festival
spots.note: Thousands of snow lanterns and floating candles line the Otaru Canal and historic streets. More intimate than Sapporo — a romantic winter escape.

spots.name: Namahage Sedo Festival
spots.note: Demonic Namahage figures (men in straw costumes and ogre masks) descend from the mountains at Shinzan Shrine by torchlight. An ancient purification tradition of Oga Peninsula.

spots.name: Sapporo White Illumination
spots.nameJa: さっぽろホワイトイルミネーション
spots.note: One of Japan's oldest winter illuminations — Odori Park and Ekimae-dori transformed with hundreds of thousands of lights. Free to enjoy.

spots.name: Ueno Park Hanami
spots.typicalDate: Late March – Early April (peak bloom dependent)
spots.note: Japan's most famous hanami spot — 1,000+ cherry trees line the paths. Blue tarp picnic parties fill every inch of space. The quintessential Tokyo sakura experience.

spots.name: Hirosaki Cherry Blossom Festival
spots.note: Japan's most beautiful sakura festival — 2,600 trees in Hirosaki Castle park. The 'petal snowstorm' (hanabuki) moment when petals fill the moat is world-famous.

spots.name: Shichi-Go-San
spots.note: Children aged 3, 5, and 7 visit shrines in kimono to pray for healthy growth. Autumn in Japan means shrines are filled with beautifully dressed children.
